citeinfo.title: Assessing spatial variability of nutrients, phytoplankton and related water-quality constituents in the California Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta at the landscape scale: 2020-2021 High-resolution mapping surveys

Assessing spatial variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water-quality constituents in the California Sacramento–San Joaquin Delta at the landscape scale—Comparison of four (2018, 2020, 2021, 2022) spring high-resolution mapping surveys

descript.abstract: The dataset documents the spatial and temporal variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water quality parameters at high spatial resolution in the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta and Suisun Bay of California, USA. The dataset includes nitrate, ammonium, orthophosphate, dissolved organic carbon, temperature, conductivity, dissolved oxygen, and chlorophyll as well as information about phytoplankton community composition. Data were collected under different environmental/flow conditions in June, July, August, and October of 2020, and May, July, and October of 2021. The data release consists of a .xml document, and .txt and shapefile documents. Descriptions for each document and file are listed in the metadata.
descript.purpose: The objective of this project is to document the variability of nutrients, phytoplankton, and related water quality parameters at high spatial resolution in the North Delta and Central Delta (California, USA).
descript.supplinf: See files: Delta Mapping 2020 and 2021 Data Dictionary.xlsx for further information regarding the datasets.

idinfo.accconst: None. Please see 'Distribution Info' for details.

Any use of trade, product, or firm names is for descriptive purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the U.S. Government.

attracc.attraccr: EXO sondes were calibrated before use in the field with the KorEXO software. For calibration, the sonde sensors are submerged one at a time in standards, then the verified chemical concentration is recorded within the instrument. If the instrument measures an unknown value of the parameter, it refers to the calibration information to calculate the concentration of the chemical in the water (EXO User Manual, pg. 81). Some software may interpret blank values as zero. Blank values should be used with caution.

dataqual.logic: Redundancy is built into the system, meaning many of the parameters included in the data set are measured by 2 different instruments. Data was displayed in real-time for on the spot user verification. Topology rules were applied to points, lines, and polygons to ensure the integrity of the geospatial data.
dataqual.complete: Data set is considered complete for the information presented, as described in the abstract. Users are advised to read the rest of the metadata record carefully for additional details.

horizpa.horizpar: We conducted a visual check that the GPS data we recorded matched the boat GPS. Data was also displayed in real-time, allowing us to spot check GPS data underway. In addition, GPS location information was compared to field notes that described known locations and features.

vertacc.vertaccr: We conducted a visual check that the GPS data we recorded matched the boat GPS. Data was also displayed in real-time, allowing us to spot check GPS data underway. In addition, GPS location information was compared to field notes that described known locations and features. Datum used NAD1983.

procstep.procdesc:
Broad-area, high-resolution, boat-based water quality mapping surveys of the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta (Delta) were conducted in May, June, July, August, and October of 2020 and 2021. Each survey was comprised of four successive days of high-resolution mapping. The regions covered included the north-south axis of the Delta from the Sacramento River north of Freeport to the Harvey Banks export facility located in the southern Delta, including Old and Middle Rivers; the east-west axis of the Delta from the Sacramento River at Rio Vista to the San Joaquin River at Stockton, including Disappointment Slough and the North and South Forks of the Mokelumne River; and the northern and western Delta from the northern reaches of the Cache Slough Complex to the Grizzly, Honker, and Suisun Bays. On each day, the route of the mapping survey was determined by weather, tides and other logistical considerations. Data were collected at high speed to minimize the extent to which tidal currents obscure nutrient and phytoplankton distributions.
Surveys were conducted using the USGS R/V Mary Landsteiner and R/V George R. Aiken on successive days, following the approach described by Fichot and others (2015), Downing and others (2016), Kimmerer and others (2019), and Stumpner and others (2020). In 2020: June 8, 9, 10, and 11; July 27 and 28; August 17, 18, and 19; and October 19, 20, 21, and 22; and in 2021: May 11, 12, 13, and 14; July 26, 27, 28, and 29; and October 4, 5, 6, and 7. For analysis, sample water was continuously pumped onto the boat using two SHURflo Aqua King II pumps at a pressure of 60 psi and rate of 5 GPM (SHURflo Pentair, London, United Kingdom) while underway at speeds up to 30 mph (13 m/s) using a pick-up tube mounted at a fixed depth of approximately 1 m below the surface. Water was routed through a 178 μm in-line strainer (Cole Parmer; EW-29595-47, Vernon Hills, Illinois) to remove large debris. Sample water was split into separate flow paths as describe below, and each flow path was routed through a pressure-compensated manifold that maintained system pressure at a proscribed level (~20 PSI) irrespective of boat speed (Downing and others, 2016). A 2-stage debubbler was used to remove bubbles can interfere with instrument performance. Flow rates were maintained by metering the discharge line, except for the flow path serving the open split interface, which was metered on the feed line. Flows were continuously monitored using a sight gauge (0.1-1 GPM Flow Meter LZM-15 with adjustable knob) for each flow path.
More detailed methods documentation can be found on the landing page (https://www.sciencebase.gov/catalog/item/62183869d34ec739b2dd292f).

Discrete water samples (DS) were collected either while underway or–in the case of more extensive sample requirements–while stopped on station. Discrete samples were collected while underway by passing water from the pickup tube through a 0.2-micron filter into a sample bottle. At designated sampling stations (approximately 10 per day) samples for determination of NH4, NO2, NO3, PO4, dissolved organic carbon (DOC), optics (absorbance and fluorescence), and total dissolved nitrogen (TDN) concentrations were collected using this system. Additionally, unfiltered samples were collected approximately from 1 m depth using a submersible pump for analysis of chlorophyll a (Chl a) concentration, pheophytin concentration, phytoplankton enumeration, and picophytoplankton enumeration. Samples for phytoplankton enumeration and picophytoplankton enumeration were fixed immediately with Lugol’s iodine solution (~5%) and formaldehyde (~2%), respectively.
Most samples were stored on wet ice in the dark during transit to the laboratory where they were stored at 2°C. Samples for picophytoplankton analysis were stored on dry ice in the dark during transit to the laboratory where picophytoplankton samples were stored at -80°C. Dissolved organic carbon samples were preserved to <2 pH with high-purity sulfuric acid the day following collection and stored at 2°C. Samples for pigment analysis were filtered within 24 hours of collection and immediately frozen (-80°C). Total Chl a samples were filtered through a 0.7 μm nominal pore size glass fiber filter (Advantec MFS, Inc, Dublin, California). Chl a associated with the larger sized cell fraction were filtered through a 5 μm pore size 25 mm polycarbonate filter (General Electric Healthcare, Chicago, Illinois). Frozen Chl a filters and chilled nutrient samples were shipped to the USGS National Water Quality Laboratory (Denver, Colorado). Phytoplankton and picophytoplankton enumeration samples were shipped to BSA Environmental Services, Inc. (Beachwood, Ohio) for analysis.
More detailed methods documentation can be found on the landing page (https://www.sciencebase.gov/catalog/item/62183869d34ec739b2dd292f).

All data, except that collected by the Timberline, were processed using the pandas software library (0.220; McKinney, 2010) in Python (3.7.3; Van Rossum and Drake, 2009) to remove periods of compromised data (e.g. flow blockages, bubbles), to apply instrument corrections and unit conversions, and to apply a centered 20 second median filter to the time series. Data from onboard continuous instruments not directly logged to the flow-through data collection system (e.g., the Timberline ammonium analyzer) were merged based on timestamp to the nearest second after estimating the differential travel times between flow paths. These time lags accounted for the additional time it took for filtered sample water to reach the instruments and the time to travel through the instruments to the detectors and for the response to be reported by the software. The time differential arises from lag introduced by filtering and specifics of instrument operation that determine the time it takes for environmental sample water to reach the instrument’s detector and for a response to be recorded.
More detailed methods documentation can be found on the landing page (https://www.sciencebase.gov/catalog/item/62183869d34ec739b2dd292f).

Data were spatially aligned to geographic coordinate system [NAD 1983 (2011)] to facilitate comparisons between dates and to minimize the effects of differential data density on the spatial interpolation calculations. The spatial alignment was performed using the GeoPandas software library in python (Jordahl, 2014), which assigned median-filtered data points to polygon shapefiles that are spaced at approximately 150 m intervals. Spatially aligned data were then aggregated by polygon shapefile to obtain summary statistics of all median-filtered data within a polygon.
Interpolated water quality data were created from the spatially aligned data using ESRI’s ArcGIS Pro Spline with Barriers tool (Terzopoulos and Witkin, 1988). The raster output was smoothed with the Focal Statistics tool, and interpolated values from the raster output were extracted to the spatially aligned points with the Extract Multi Values to Points tool to create continuous spatially aligned data across the mapping domain. Rasters were converted to vector format using the Contour tool, then clipped with the Clip tool using a Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta polygon obtained from the National Hydrography Dataset (U.S. Geological Survey, 2016). These data are referred to as spatially aligned (SA) and are associated with a specific sampling day, and water quality parameter.
More detailed methods documentation can be found on the landing page (https://www.sciencebase.gov/catalog/item/62183869d34ec739b2dd292f).

enttyp.enttypd: Definitions of column headers for 2020-2021 Delta Mapping data release

overview.eaover: The data dictionary is titled Delta Water Quality Mapping 2020 and 2021 Data Dictionary. This file lists and describes the labels in tables.
